description: Thursday, 18 June 2026 | Primary Production Committee Hearings of Evidence: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Agriculture, Biosecurity, Fisheries and Food Safety Minister of Agriculture Hon Todd McClay Ministry for Primary Industries Ray Smith, Director-General Gillon Carruthers, DDG Public Affairs Karen Adair, DDG Agriculture and Investment Services Sam Keenan, DDG Te Uru Rākau – New Zealand Forest Service Glenn Webber, DDG Māori Partnerships and Investments Julie Collins, DDG Policy & Trade Neil Cherry, DDG Corporate Services John Roche, Chief Science Advisor 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Forestry Minister for Forestry Hon Todd McClay Ministry for Primary Industries Ray Smith, Director-General Gillon Carruthers, DDG Public Affairs Karen Adair, DDG Agriculture and Investment Services Sam Keenan, DDG, NZ Forest Service Glenn Webber, DDG Māori Partnerships and Investments Julie Collins, DDG Policy & Trade Neil Cherry, DDG Corporate Services John Roche, Chief Science Advisor
descriptionSummary: Hearings of Evidence. 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Agriculture, Biosecurity, Fisheries and Food Safety. Minister of Agriculture

chunks.text: Media standing everywhere Oh, yeah. So we had to go and tell them off before. I know, but I told them that they're wasting their time.

Are you all right? I was going to say, are they leaving? What did I do? Have you thrown your toys? No, I've got another. You've got another sleeve. It's got to be there. Yeah. Okay. Thanks, Steve. Okay. I'm going to go. I just have to go to a different committee.

Right. Good morning, Minister. Great to have you here. Thank you. Thank you so much. You're welcome. Fire away. No, you first. So you want to hear some comments from me and then questions? Yes, please. Any day. All right. Well, thanks so much. Well, colleagues, thank you very much for the invite in. Can I recognize, I think all of us were at the field days last week, and what a celebration of the exceptional nature of New Zealand agriculture. The interesting thing about the field days, it's not only about the farmers and their animals or what they grow, but I think it increasingly

becomes a fantastic demonstration of the innovation that sits behind the New Zealand primary sector. And so, it was good to see so many farmers and growers out enjoying themselves and in a better place than they have been previously. You all right? Did you just go round there and come back?

Just conflicts because we've got Jones next door and Ozzy in there. Good. Welcome back. All righty. So a couple of things I'll touch on and then, of course, we've got plenty of time for comments and questions. First is we released last week the situation out for the primary industries. There's the forecast for the next year, and the primary sector continues to deliver for the New Zealand economy.

Expected exports up 6% to hit $64 billion for the very first time. Estimated to grow to $70 billion by 2030. I think it'll actually be higher than that, but it's just positive news and all in the right direction. The standouts I think were dairy up 5%, a record of $28.6 billion worth of exports.

Meat and wool up 14%, which is extremely good news for beef and sheep farmers. A record $14.1 billion worth of exports. And hort up 7%, 9.5 billion. Kiwifruit, the standout I think within that is 16%. The kiwifruit sector is getting great returns all around the world, almost every market, whilst at the same time they're increasing their production.

Processed food was up 5%, 3.5 billion, which of course is where there's additional value being added. The other thing I want to mention is the state of the sector and the men and women that work in the primary industry. When we came to government, confidence levels in agriculture through public surveys were as low as -66%. In July of last year, they were +33%, which is a significant increase.

It's a combination of things, and you've got to remember that we've had adverse weather events, which is challenging for farmers and growers. But I think it is on the back of farmers feeling valued, working hard. We've worked hard to simplify rules, to make it easier for them to meet their obligations, because they do have to meet obligations.

But at the same time, they're getting better returns in international markets. From a trade point of view, without good trade access, our farmers and growers wouldn't be able to produce all that they do. 83% of all of our goods exports come from the primary sector. More than 15% of GDP is from food and fiber. Some standouts are now the UK FTA is now three years in operation. We've seen almost a 79% increase in exports to the UK during that period of time, with the primary sector doing a lot of the heavy lifting.

The EU FTA is now two years in force, with an extra $3 billion worth of exports, again, predominantly from the primary sector. And a few other areas of interest, the UAE FTA, which on entry to force warrants 99% liberalization, saw a 34% increase in exports in the first six months.

That's been moderated as a result of war in the Middle East, but there's still a very strong demand. You may well have noticed tariff rates from the US, and that has been a confusing and changing sort of story. We started at 10% on liberation day, went up to 15% as a result of us having a very modest surplus.

That was found to be illegal. It went back to 10%, and the US has just announced that it's likely to go to 12.5%, which in itself is less than most exporters thought. They expect it may go back to 15%. But even with all of that, we've been able to get our beef, kiwifruit, and a few other things back down to the equivalent of a zero tariff rate. And our exports to the US have gone up by about 4% by value over that period of time We now have greater market access and demand for what we produce from the primary sector, which is, I

suppose, a good position to be in because our exporters have options as to where they put their product. But we face the challenge of how we meet that growing demand for the high quality and safe food and fiber we produce in New Zealand. Last week at the field days, we announced a new scheme and policy called Land Use Flexibility, helping Kiwi farmers and growers to feed the world. And as we have more market access, the free trade agreement with India is an example, which has the potential to be as important to New Zealand's economy and our

primary sector as China is, how can we increase our production whilst keeping that very high quality for safe food that are environmentally consciousness. And so ultimately, consumers around the world like the products that come from New Zealand. They like the story that backs that up, but ultimately they have choices. So this land use flexibility says to farmers that actually the government is going to co-invest in projects to test out how we can improve environmental and climate outcomes whilst increasing production. And, behind that, we

announced six projects which are co-funded in the private sector, in dairy, red meat, forestry, in aquaculture, in horticulture, and around Maori growers. As an example, in dairy, the project aims to reduce nitrate leaching by 20% whilst increasing efficiency and productivity on farm.

On red meat sector, there are pilots will be underway, where the use of collars aims to increase after utilization by 20%, thus creating more red meat with the same amount of land and so on. I would say that actually we have an opportunity to feed the world and grow the New Zealand economy.

It cannot be at the detriment of the environment or climate change. The two have to go together. We have the ability, as we've seen at the field day, to continue to innovate and the government with the private sector see this as a partnership. Finally, in this area around catchment groups, which again looks at the opportunity around better management of resources and water.

We've announced $40.5 million over the next four years, an increase of $4.5 million this year. We support about 51 catchment groups at the moment, well, actually 51, and we're looking to establish 17 and support 17 collectives to sit over the top. I think where farmers join together in these catchment groups with decision makers, you get a much, much better outcome, and we're looking to encourage that. The budget itself, which is before you, budget this year allocated $4.77 billion over four years to the Ministry of Primary Industries.

We've found savings of, we announced savings of $13.2 million per year, which we've been able to do. We've also had an increase of $119 million around aquaculture settlements, $170 million for wilding pines, a small $2.2 million to help the ministry get ready for the India FTA implementation, and $47 million for other settlements.

Two final bits I'll touch on, and I'm sure we can talk more in questions. You will be aware that McCain's has announced the closure of a plant in the Hawke's Bay, which has created concern. I've been to visit the region and met with the mayors on a number of occasions to have conversations.

There are a group of vegetable growers there who are exploring whether a private purchase or cooperative may work in the place of McCain's exiting. We've committed $50,000 to help with the early stages of feasibility study to see whether that business model could be put together. The growers themselves, with the council and others, are currently working through that. I would caution that I think it's very, very difficult, but the government views it, we have a role to play to support those growers in the meantime to see where they get to.

I'm going to be very cautious in what I would say around that because there are commercial discussions and negotiations going on at the moment, and I certainly don't want to get into the middle of those. But they have made a reasonable case as to their view that they think there could be a future for a plant there in the Hawke's Bay, and so we're happy to join with them, to work through what that could look like.

But I would also caution it's very, very early days. Finally, on Maori agribusiness, we in the budget have money allocated to continue to support Maori enterprises. One of the very big challenges I think we do have is around underutilized Maori or multiple iwi land. It's why Mike Buttrick is an associate minister.

I've given him responsibility for Maori agriculture with a particular focus on working with landowners to try and find ways to free up land and get better access to that. Ultimately, about 10% of New Zealand agricultural land is Maori owned. It contributes about $12 billion directly in output and employs a lot of New Zealanders, including a lot of young Maori New Zealanders.

So you'll see a greater focus on land utilization from the government with Mike's giving this particular specific responsibility. We hope to see other things that we can do as we move forward to help with better utilization of iwi land. With that in mind, I've got pages more, but why don't I stop for a while?

Fantastic. Right. Well, I know we've got a question from Steve. Thank you, Madam Chair. And, just a quick note, I appreciate your finding 50K to support the bid for a growers' cooperative for McCain. I think they probably need to find a bit more than that, but it's a step in the right direction. As to your expenditure you referred to on- The deal that was done and announced at Fieldays around the $18 million contributing to the Dairy New Zealand project around improving outcomes.

One question I've got for you is that given how well dairy is doing, aren't they wealthy enough and big enough to be investing in that stuff themselves? It's a good project, but why is there $18 million of public money going towards that when we're losing arable farmers in Canterbury?

And shouldn't the arable sector be being better supported through public money? Well, I'm not going to get into a debate about those you think are wealthy and what they should be paying. I think overall, the government has funding available and good projects come forward that will help us meet environmental obligations or grow the economy, which is good for all New Zealanders.

We've considered them. We don't want to be a government that necessarily sits there and says, "You're too wealthy, let's take off you," because that would end up with tax- No, but you're putting public money. Yeah. You're investing- Let me finish. I didn't interrupt you. So we can either have question answer or have a debate. We should do that in Parliament, right? So ultimately, you are right about arable.

So the other reason why I've asked Mike Butcher to take on responsibility is the allegation for arable farming. They are finding it more challenging. There are a range of reasons for that at the moment. The first is prices around the world are down because of oversupply, firstly. Secondly, they face challenges down in Canterbury, particularly because of a number of weather events.

And then thirdly, ultimately, they have had costs put on them. Some are to do with input costs from overseas, others are to do with very, very restrictive rules in Canterbury around what they can do and how they can do it upon their land. You'll know that through the RMA and other areas, we're working through to simplify rules and make them more user-friendly whilst ensuring environmental protection.

But there is support that goes into arable farming, and it is a priority. Why have we done that for dairy? Well, the dairy, you're right, is a significant contributor to the New Zealand economy. It's a very important part of our export profile. Although only one part of our export profile, we think that we can get the environmental gains more quickly, whilst improving production by partnering with the sector, Steve, not telling them we think they are wealthy and therefore they should pay themselves.

Can I do a follow-up? Yes, sure. In terms of the ambition around reducing nitrate, which is a good ambition. Mm-hmm. How do you contend with the fact that we're getting an expansion of dairy now in Canterbury, looking at 40,000 new cows, and the primary problem with nitrate leachate is the sheer volume of nutrient going into catchments.

So aren't you failing to appropriately manage that volume of nutrient when you're allowing further expansion of an already very extensive, very intensive form of farming? So the two are not linked, by the way. This is a new policy going forward. The other is existing policy.

So land use flexibility is about saying to farmers, rather than a heavy-handed approach through regulation and councils as to what you can and can't do with your land. If you can show an improved environmental outcome, then actually you should have flexibility to look for the better value from your land. Let me give you an example, which I think comes from Canterbury, but I'd need to check to be entirely sure.

I went to, visited a group of farms a while ago, a dairy farm that farmer had the other side of the road, a beef lot. In winter, he was not allowed to put his dairy cows there, even though there would be a better environmental outcome because his soil was better, because it would be viewed as a land use change with the potential for dairy. So, the rules were getting in the way in a better environmental outcome. So land use flexibility is about saying, "Actually, we want better rules. We want an improvement when it comes to the

environment." But the way to do that is technology and innovation, not closing down farms. Your point about Canterbury is not an unreasonable one, but ultimately, that's one of the reasons that we're going through replacement of the RMA, because we want better certainty for everybody involved. And by the way, some of those arable farmers that are finding it very, very hard to make money on their land are looking for other types of land use, some of which is likely to be dairy.

Exactly. Yeah. Thank you. Joy, you had a question. Thank you, Minister. I wanted to ask you a question with regard to the baseline savings of 13.2 million per annum over a period of years. Can you tell us exactly how you're going to, or the ministry is going to achieve that reduction, and what specific activities or functions will be cut or lost as a result of that?

Yeah, sure. I'll pass to Ray Smith in a moment, who may be going to go into a bit more detail. But we're very clear in looking for these savings, that I've been very clear and very insistent that actually there should be no cuts and reduction of service at the front line. And that's very similar to the process we went through two years ago as well. And so ultimately, as with many New Zealanders and many, many farmers who have to look at ways to drive efficiencies within our organization.

They are doing it every day within their business. How can we achieve more with the same or a reduced amount of effort or input? How can we be much more efficient? But I want to be very clear again, I've asked the department to ensure that the delivery of frontline services remains the same. And actually, over the last two years, I think we've seen a significant increase in effort and activity, both through organizational structure, but also necessity as weather events and other things have happened in biosecurity from MPI. But Ray, maybe you want to go in a bit

more detail. Yeah, sure. Just first thing says there's no change to frontline. Service. And if you think about the 13 million, just think about it in the context of $1.3 billion budget. So, to quite honestly, these are largely going to be through administrative savings that we can make through the things we can do in the back office areas or around the contracts we have in here and with IT systems or administrative things that we operate.

So, our staff won't really notice any difference as we go through this. Whether it's getting a gain on a property lease or whatever it is that we're trying to do. At this level, that level of savings is not going to impact any of the frontline services.

So I'm very confident about that. In fact, we haven't initiated any sort of reorganizing anything on the basis of that number. So, sorry, just a minute. So, the second part to my question was around what specific activities and functions will be cut. I mean, aside from just- Office space house... okay, reduced, sorry, I should say.

So, I see that a large chunk of the reduction is in the policy advice and ministerial servicing. So how will you ensure that OIA responses and WPQ responses are received on time? And I'd be really interested to know if you'd share what your rate of on time responses are.

Yeah, they've been very good. It's something I've said to the ministry, irrespective of the number of parliamentary questions you may get and our view of how helpful they may be or OIAs, the rules are clear, the law is the law, so we work to adhere to those. There are occasions where we miss deadlines, and there'd be a range of reasons for that. But we have a view of getting OIAs out as quickly as we can.

And I've got to thank the ministry because we get a very significant number, and they have a great team that works upon that. But again, there are many ways where you can draw efficiencies within many offices, including mine. We've looked at the processes we have in place, how many people are involved in that, how we can get decisions more quickly as to information needed and OIAs going out and so on.

But Ray, I don't know if there's more you want to add to that. Yeah, it is a big overhead cost for government agencies because the number of OIAs and parliamentary questions continues to increase. Mm-hmm. And at quite a rapid rate, I have to say. Mm. And so we have absorbed a lot there. I'm not concerned about our ability to do it.

I think we sit around about the 95% mark on OIAs. But perhaps more important statistic in there or one to look alongside, if you sort of, as I'm trying to gauge how we're going, is the complaints that are made to the Ombudsman and how many of those are upheld. And I think you'll note that we have, I think, only one or two out of the, and the number of complaints come down.

So even if we sometimes kind of miss that first deadline, we make sure that we do what complainants or requesters are asking, try to get that right. On the PG side, I think we're pretty good. But there are large volumes, and all of us in the public service are going to have to think about as we go forward. We can't have an ever-increasing number of these things without some rethinking about how we deal with responding to them.

Yeah. Can I have that one sec? Yep, thank you. How many staff positions will be affected? Well, none- From the 13.2 million reduction? None specifically from the 13 point. We've currently got 3,467 staff, I think as at the end of May on board.

That's roughly what we had last year. Our projection was always that that will fall slightly. So that's now bottom-team forecast as we find administrative efficiencies. So we're not targeting any particular areas to take people out of, but we have been operating slightly below what we could afford to operate with, with staffing numbers, and anticipation of the fact that we would have to continue to make savings.

There has been no problem with that in terms of the services we provided. So I feel that we had planned forward well on anticipating the fact that we'd have to make these savings, and so we're in quite a good position. So you won't see us heading off on a major restructuring to try and achieve the savings you want.

We might go to it. There's a lot- I've got more subs if I can come back. Oh, no, we have one more, and then we'll go to some others. Is it a sub or a new primary? No, it's around jobs and things. Mm. Is the ministry's Biosecurity New Zealand business unit exempt from public service number job cuts?

No. It's not. Well, nothing is exempt other than that which has been stated by the government, which is there are a range of departments that are in different categories. I think that was part of the original announcement. But the thing to think about when you think about NPI's business is all of those frontline border security roles are not funded through taxpayer funds.

Mm-hmm. They're funded through levies that are collected through passengers or through cargo. And so, in a sense, they're not part of that scheme. So when we think about frontline staff there, and that's about 700 odd frontline staff work in those frontline border roles. They are funded through a different mechanism.

And we're happy with the funding that was there because we just reduced the fees from sort of $16 to $12, I think, on the airport passenger levy. Likewise, food safety is about 70 or 80% cost recovered. So again, businesses pay for the services they get, and that's how the staff are paid for. So in terms of biosecurity overall, I'm not concerned about any of those frontline areas.

But biosecurity, like all of the other business groups in NPI, all have functions in the head office with me here in Wellington as well. And that's where collectively we can probably make more gains So that comes to the point where I said very clear that in looking for these efficiencies, actually, the frontline service should not be affected. But there is a difference between what happens at the back office, and then those who are out on the frontline.

So ultimately, if you go back to a period of time, I think under the previous government, we saw a very large increase in the number of people across the civil service, but not an equal amount by way of percentage on the frontline. We've been very focused in this direction that says where efficiency be made, they should be back of office.

Frontline, actually, where we're able to or where there's necessity, as you've seen from time to time when there's been a pest infiltration or something, the resources and people are there to deal with that. Just seeking quick clarification. Very quick. So you're saying they're not exempt from the government's public service broad number cuts? So I'll answer this. So you're looking for a headline that says, "Ah, they're going No, I'm not. I'm simply asking a question, Minister.

So ultimately... That's good. So well, it feels like you are because we've answered that, but I can be very, very clear. No, I just wanted to clarify that. That's all. Well, I'm pretty sure it was answered very, very clearly. It doesn't need to be exempt because ministers have the ability to make decisions for themselves. And as previously, as I'm saying again now, there will be no reduction or cuts to frontline services over the areas that you're speaking of.

That doesn't mean there won't be changes to the back office. But ultimately, when I talk to farmers, they're very keen to make sure that the border is secure, and they probably are happy for us to reorganize to some degree internally in the back office, as long as the frontline is not there. But we didn't need to go and seek an exemption for the frontline because we're given a very, very clear commitment over the last three years to increase support in that area, not decrease.

Thank you, Minister. We've got a question from Dana. Thank you. Thank you, Minister. Look, firstly, can I say congratulations on a fine piece of work with the Indian FTA and what that will mean for our primary production exporters and producers. But what I'm interested in understanding more of is how is MPI supporting those farmers, exporters, producers, orchardists, to take advantage of the new trade agreements, specifically India, but of course others as well.

Well, there's a couple of things there. The first is, so for an exporter, put the tariff reductions and quotas and so on aside for a moment, although we've been very fortunate with our negotiators to get things others around the world no one else has had before. One of the more important things we can do is help exporters from the primary sector get their goods to market a lot more quickly.

So within the free trade agreement, there are commitments on both sides, Australia, India, and New Zealand, that perishable goods should clear their border within 24 hours or hours, and non-perishable within 48 hours. And that would be a significant improvement from some exporters and what they've faced over periods of time. MPI play a very important role in that, both in ensuring certification of the product that goes to market is recognized, but they actually work very, very closely with their counterparts in India and other nations

to make sure the system works well and to build levels of recognition and trust. We have people in market from MPI, and in fact, actually, the number of, a couple of places around the world, extra posts have been established to put more people there. They are doing the day-to-day business of helping our exporters as they have products cross the border.

And they're also actually where challenges may be created, are able to act specifically to try and work that through the system. But maybe there's more you'd like to add. Oh, I mean, I think we're in a pretty positive place in terms of trade and market access around the world. And probably the big, I think the challenge that's going to emerge for farmers and growers in New Zealand is trying to fulfill all of those opportunities that exist in those trade agreements now. And it's very diversified.

And you can see that if you look into the SOPI results. You can see increasingly people are able to, or companies in particular, are able to take advantage of a whole range of different markets now. And so India's going to just give us a kind of an even bigger diversification. Just a supplementary on that, thank you. Are you confident that with the increased trade, that the certification or quality assurance at the port in terms of- Oh... assessing- Yeah... shipments or containers going out will be able to keep up?

Oh, 100%. So again, the food safety system operates on almost 70 or 80% cost recovery. So if you're running a food business in New Zealand, whether you're a Fonterra or a small business, it doesn't matter. You pay for the services that are required. So all of that certification is paid for by the companies, and so we just adjust our staffing levels to meet the demand as is needed. So yeah, I'm not concerned about that.

Thank you. I've got a quick sup on that. It's an exciting opportunity for lamb farmers as well, obviously, sheep farmers. And I just wonder, what's this secure-- is there a secure cold chain? Is it just for frozen? Do they just take frozen meat at this stage or? No, they can take either there. Or are we working on that? And so into market, it is very, very clear. It's the same as we do to other countries.

Then in country, that's very much to do with the exporters and the arrangements that they have with the importers and so on. But it's evolving very, very quickly. Yeah. So you look at fruit as an example, where we have had fruit go in there, although it's been very challenging because extremely high tariff rates. With the new FTA in place, there's a lot of talk about investment in the cold chain there. There are New Zealand companies are looking forward to going and playing a part in that, giving advice or help with the construction and

things like that. Some have said they want to go and establish in India themselves, which by the way, would be an investment where New Zealanders and Indians are employed to help grow the economy. And so ultimately, it's a very large country. It's a little bit like some parts of Southeast Asia have been previously. Yeah. This will evolve and develop over time as the amount of product that comes from New Zealand flows and increases.

Fantastic. Thank you. Right. Maureen. Thank you, Madam Chair. Minister, to show the congratulations on the work that you've been doing for New Zealand. I want to bring my question home a little bit, which is about the agricultural recovery assistance. Mm. That's more than halved in this appropriation, and I just wondered if you've got special intel that tells you that weather events are going to reduce, or have you got a different way of distributing that, and how will you target given the reduced amount?

Yeah. So funding as it is needed is made available. We've increased our funding in a number of areas, including in our field days last week, announced additional funding to get people ready for weather events. So part of it, we're also doing it as, not only just when things happen, but how can we make sure farmers and growers are well prepared, because there are going to be more.

And so, that investment is going into some farm leaders in areas and different organizations and so on, and so preemptively getting them there. But generally, so the fund is available for us to use where we need it. It comes from a number of places across government, but the funds we have available to us, I think are an area that we need.

Yeah. A quick sup? Yes, sure. Thank you. So if this particular fund is oversubscribed, what you're saying is there will be money for farmers- Yes... if needed. That's right. So very much a case-by-case basis. So we have the ability within the budget to move money around with contingencies. A better example probably is around biosecurity. So rather than having an amount of money, say this is for what might happen, right, which means you can't use it elsewhere. We have the ability to seek additional funding within our own budget or go back to Cabinet where something

of an extreme nature has happened. But the point that I'd make is, yes, we have the relevant funds that we need, and should we need more, we have the ability to allocate that. It's very reassuring. Thank you. Mm-hmm. Thank you, Scott. Thank you. Thank you, Minister. How much of the 2026/27 funding allocation for the Primary Sector Growth Fund is expected to support climate adaptation and resilience compared or against increasing production and output?

So, we've made some changes to the fund last year to focus and target it more. Those who seek funding for the government have to put more in for themselves, and we think it's the way that we can get a better use of taxpayer money across many more projects. It is based upon the projects that are put in, and they are all treated equally. We have an independent panel that assesses them and gives advice to the ministry that then provides it to me.

And so ultimately, any proposal that comes from an organization, a private sector, a business, that meets the criteria of their amount of funding versus ours, and the criteria that set has an equal ability to receive funding. So we don't allocate a certain amount that says it will be for this part of the private sector or somewhere else, anything like that.

It is very much on a first come, first served basis as long as they meet the requirement. However, in other areas, we have funding that is made available that are not necessarily through the Primary Sector Growth Fund. You would've seen last week that we made announcements around a climate example with Ag Zero, that $400 million that we're investing to help find the solutions for farmers to reduce their methane emissions and other things without reducing production. And we're able to announce a $100 million program of which the government is putting in half, to help

uptake on farm for those sorts of things. So there's a range of areas that funding are available, things we've asked, but as far as the Primary Sector Growth Fund is concerned, it is very much based upon the projects that are, or applications. And if I could follow. What assurance can the minister give us that the Primary Sector Growth Fund will not simply become a taxpayer-funded mechanism to increase intensification?

I can give you every assurance that you require. But in as far as intensification is concerned, you've got to be very careful of that word. Yeah. Finding ways to produce more of the same amount of less, some would argue is intensification. I would view that as joining farmers to do what they've done very effectively and very efficiently for a very long period of time. So if you take an example of, I opened a factory a small while ago in Taupō, where innovation has come. They're taking fertilizer, mainly nitrogen.

They've found a new way to coat it so it releases more slowly over months rather than very, very quickly. There's a greater utilization of that nitrogen. It's about twice the cost of traditional fertilizer, but you get a much better utilization of it, the grass or trees going to grow better, and there is much less leaching.

That in itself would allow an increase of production on the land. Some may call that intensification, but actually, as I was saying earlier, maybe before you were able to come in, the land use flexibility project that we've launched is about finding ways to increase what we produce, while having the same or an improved environmental footprint, and that's using innovations.

So in all cases- I see the funds that have been used around the Primary Sector Growth Fund to help get better outcomes for the primary sector, and in some cases, we would hope it would mean more production through being greatly more efficient. Thank you. Just quickly follow up on that.

Very quickly, yes. So if every project under the Primary Sector Growth Fund is required to demonstrate measurable environmental benefit, is it possible for a project to receive public funding despite increasing environmental pressure? So there are a range of things. So as an example, there's a project with beef and lamb to work on a medium to long term, find other solutions for the management of facial eczema on sheep and cattle.

And so what we know as a result of facial eczema, production is less, animal welfare concerns go up as animal health goes down. And so we're partnering with them. It's a significant body of work over an extended period of time, and if we could only achieve some of what the project is aiming to do, production goes up and animal welfare goes up. Now that in itself isn't environmental, but it has an animal welfare component. There are other parts are purely about finding better ways to produce. There's an example, of course, of funding we've just put in a

short time ago in dairy farming, which is around dairy and beef together. Taking the bobby calf not needed and creating some other value for them in the beef herd. And that in itself will drive greater efficiency should it be suitable. You're taking another part of the production chains that has little less value, or some people would view sometimes as a cost, and actually adding value to it. So it's a very wide broad range of things that are considered.

Some and many are environmental, but they're not all. Thank you, Minister. Oh, we have a question from Paulo. Minister, good morning, and I just wanted to take this opportunity to thank you and your team for all the fantastic work that you do, and that you've brought New Zealand to such a high level of trade and export.

Minister, could you speak to the government's key priorities in supporting and sustaining this growth? You're over 80% already. What are your key priorities? Yeah. Well, first and foremost, whether it's the trade agreements we do, the work that we do, through MPI or the Primary Sector Growth Fund, it's about driving up returns at the farm gate for our growers and our farmers.

They have a huge contribution to New Zealand and the economy. They're a very important part of the New Zealand economy. 360,000 men and women are employed because of the primary sector, and we have opportunity to grow that. The reputation that New Zealand farmers and growers have around the world is exceptional. We've got to be very careful we don't talk ourselves down compared to almost every other country that we've seen or I've been able to visit. Actually, what we do in New Zealand is exceptional standard.

I think that's the reason you're seeing greater returns for the amount of effort farmers put in through customers and consumers all around the world. So we want a very strong primary sector. They want to meet their obligations. They have them around environment, animal welfare, climate change.

But we have to have rules that are fit for purpose to do that, and if a rule that comes from Wellington merely adds cost and doesn't achieve what we need to, well, that's bureaucracy. That's what red tape is. So there has never been a discussion over the last three years about not meeting obligations, about not improving environmental outcomes, about not finding ways to achieve more, and to produce more. The two have to go hand in hand together.

And the reason for that is that the reputation that we have helps products be chosen overseas over those from other like-minded similar countries we might be compared to. We're focused, over the last three years, on simplifying these rules, but simplifying means easier to achieve what is needed rather than just adding cost. And that work should always continue as farmers continue to look for ways to improve what they do to be more effective, to be more efficient, to reduce costs, to be more careful about what they spend, to produce more whilst caring about the

environment and selling more overseas. The more we get to sell overseas, the greater the return, the better off every New Zealander is. Yeah. Minister, predictions for the future for primary industries? Look, I'm extremely optimistic. I think there has not been for a long time, a better time to be a farmer or a grower in New Zealand or anywhere in the world.

And the face of significant headwinds from tariff rates and change in the US to input costs that have gone up because of the war in the Middle East to other types of uncertainty and so on, our farmers have year on year, doing well, doing extremely well for New Zealand, as others are. The demand for protein continues to increase.

Part of the reason you are seeing good dairy returns, I am told, and you'll see newspaper articles around this if you look around the world, is the advent of the desire for people to be healthier as they lose weight. And when you get Starbucks in America buying dairy protein to add to their coffee so it has higher protein in it, it tells me that our farmers are in a pretty good place.

But it's not only dairy. You've seen meat prices increase as demand has. This is not just about commodity anymore. In China, I might have mentioned when I was here last year before you, one of the meat companies there has launched a burger that is ready to eat, ready to heat, ready to cook, and the value is added in New Zealand. And the return they're getting for that, along with many other products with the value added here, with that New Zealand grass-fed standard put on it that the government has developed by sector and is promoting, is exceptional.

And so there's a lot of interest. The final that I would touch on is it's very broad for the kiwi fruit that is going great returns for New Zealand, and it's called kiwi fruit, not Chilean fruit or Australian fruit or European fruit. It's Chilean fruit. But I want to make you think about the wool industry. When we came to government three years ago, sheep farmers were hardly making money on their lamb, and they were losing money in wool, and there were suggestions that the wool industry would be over. Actually now, they're making-

Money again. They're getting a greater return than their cost. That's a number of things. The price has gone up. There are greater utilizations of wool being used, and last week we announced $8 million, I think it was, for the Primary Sector Growth Fund to help the next stage to develop other uses of the wool itself, from pigments to dyes, to all sorts of things like this, which is a greater value than the wool itself, to people seeking out New Zealand wool because it's strong to add to garments and carpets around the world.

The India free trade agreement goes to a zero tariff rate for wool on day one. When I was there, the day before the signing, I met in a meeting a man who has a carpet mill. There's a chance his carpet mill produces more carpet than the whole of New Zealand does. He sells in India and the countries around India. He uses some New Zealand wool because of the strong nature to blend with theirs.

When I met with him, he said, "With the tariff rate coming to zero, I am only going to use New Zealand wool because of its quality." And if he is producing carpets and rugs that are of a premium value, then whilst it will be a commercial negotiation as to what you pay for the wool from New Zealand, if he doesn't have the wool, he can't make the carpet to sell.

So I think across the board, farmers are right to be feeling good. They are right to be smiling at field days, and I thank them for their hard work. We've all got to just work hard to keep backing them, keep rules simple, so they can meet their obligations to keep selling the highest quality, safest product in the world. Thank you. I've got a quick sup. So do you think the focus on reducing red tape and increasing productivity, it's good for farmers and it's good for growth, but it also has a flow on effect for New Zealand consumers?

If you're reducing the cost of production, it's good for consumers too. Yeah. So certainly where you can get cost out of a system, as you've heard us say around the economies we're going to make in the primary MPI budget, finding efficiencies is not a bad thing. Mm-hmm. Organizations, companies, businesses that keep doing what they've always done and adding cost, in the end, go out of business. And government, if you keep doing that, you have to put taxes up, where a government that doesn't want to increase taxes, we want to be

very careful with taxpayer resource. So the answer is yes. It is very hard for a government to do something about what farmers earn. We can do free trade agreements and give them better access, but actually, prices often are set internationally. We can help them add value and to promote to be chosen first. That grass-fed standard is a very good example of that.

But ultimately, international markets often will set a price. Sure. We have an absolute obligation to focus on the costs that are created and put upon businesses, and in this case, farmers. And so we've been looking to find efficiencies and better ways to achieve what we need to. But it's not just about the cost to consumers and whether that's being passed on or not. You get better environment and climate outcomes when you work with people and you find solutions.

I want to use the example, just because it is one that was raised with me in a meeting in my electorate yesterday, of the Ute Tax. There is a desire, many more people are purchasing electric vehicles at the moment. But taxes change behavior, and if you want somebody to alter their behavior with a tax, they have to have an alternative to be able to do it. In the case of Ute Tax, where there wasn't an electric vehicle that could replace it, it just became a tax that either is a cost to the business or it gets passed on.

There are better ways to find efficiencies when it comes to the utilization of fuel and other things and so on. Some of the projects that farmers are doing themselves of how can I use my tractor less to do the same amount of work to reduce a cost? And that's just something the government should always focus on and continue to work with, so.

Thank you. I've got a question from Jo. Thank you. I just want to come back to the savings that the ministry is looking to find. And I note that some may be found by ending or scaling back low-value programs. I just want to know, what criteria or evidence are you going to be looking at or using to determine what has been a low-value program that you will no longer continue to?

Well, I'll let Ray answer that in detail, but my expectation is projects that are taking too long and are not achieving what they need to, or the money can be better utilized elsewhere within the system to drive a better outcome more quickly, is an example of that. So if I come back to the Primary Sector Growth Fund, it's had a number of iterations. It started a long time ago when we were last in government. The name was changed with a change of government under Labor, and I've had a look at it.

And so what we've been able to do there is say all the projects are worthy, but actually, some of them, the government feels like the taxpayer has a greater share than they might. And I'm a firm believer in the case of these projects is those that are asking us to support them should have more skin in the game rather than merely socializing risk.

There is sometimes a need for governments to do things that others would argue might not, to try and get that next idea that's going to help the sector to solve the facial eczema problem, as an example. But what we did within that was, rather than reducing the amount we spent or canning it, refocused it for the outcome, so that to the short to medium term, there is more likely to be a greater return at the farm gate for the farmer. But also then saying, but other projects that take longer that actually could have a greater positive effect upon agriculture as a

whole, then they could too be supported. But maybe there's some specific detail. Yeah, no. I think- We spent a lot of money. It's a large budget. We buy a lot of services, and we buy a lot of services from a lot of people, and we will continue to seek to make changes to who we buy from so we get the best value.

And we've made some adjustments over time, where I think we've brought new service providers on that have given us better value for money than others. You'd expect us to do that. And so there's always gains that you can make in terms of the value you're getting for your money. And when I think in an overall sense, there's those services that are very directly delivering something every day to frontline consumers, and then there's a whole range of more discretionary elements that all of us have in large departments. And so you look into those

and you go, "Which ones are giving us the greatest gains?" But we wouldn't make any sort of substantive adjustment to a program we were delivering without first a discussion with the government or with ministers. So I think in this whole savings program as we go forward, the process is probably quite important to understand.

My job is to go back to ministers in the next month or two with some of the ideas we've got about how we could make more efficiencies. Those things will be considered by ministers, and then ultimately, probably at Cabinet for some of those things across the whole public service. And then, ultimately, that'll lay out a bit of a plan for some of the initiatives that we'll take on savings. So there's quite a ways to go yet before we take any direct initiatives. The savings that are already in train that have come from previous budgets and so on, those are already well

planned for. Mm. And as I said, they largely have been able to be taken out through administrative cost. And so are you yet to make decisions about which programs may end as a result of- Could be- Finding savings... could be programs. It tends to be initiatives rather than big programs that are currently running.

It's not often you see those. But it tends to be initiatives that the department might be undertaking of working on, or things where the government might consider actually that money could be spent on a different priority. So we'll work together where we think our budget lines are and what we think we would like to take on in the year where we've got some discretion.

And that'll be a conversation we have with ministers over the coming months. There's another part of this, which is around how do you get more for the money that you have. The $1.33 billion we'll spend this year, $13.2 million is important. But perhaps if we can find savings and accommodations, we continue to do this. So if we take our biosecurity as an example, you've seen us announce $70 million additional to go into wilding pines because the case has been made that actually there is a growing problem there.

But before we got to the point of deciding on a quantum of additional resource for wilding pines, I asked the ministry to go through what we were doing at the moment and how more effectively and efficiently we could use that money to get rid of more wilding pines. Mm. Because more money in a problem, if the system is not efficient, creates greater inefficiency, and it takes resource away from somewhere else that you might use it. If you think about wallabies, whereas in Canterbury and around Maiot, Ruatāhuna, there is a large

growing population. We've asked the question of how much are we spending, how many animals are we getting, how can we do that better? Don't do a big expensive study to tell me that. Go and find a way to do it better. So there's two things we continue to do. How can we find savings? Are there projects that actually, as they come to an end, we don't need to renew them because they have or haven't achieved what we need? Is there spending that actually could be reprioritized to achieve much more, or there are savings equally that we can find through greater efficiency?

So, projects start and finish all the time. If we take Jobs for Nature, which was under the last government, it was two and bound. It was four years. They were always estimated to finish at the end of that. And so that's just the nature of governments making decisions as to where you spend resource. If you don't make decisions like that, budgets go up, and therefore taxes have to go up, and I don't think anybody wants that.

One area, too, maybe just to add useful insight, and it's quite well covered in the papers is, if you look at contractor and consultant spend. Mm. We will probably finish up at about 7 million this year. It was 10 million last year. If you look back through the years, it sort of peaked up in the 40s. So, we've really worked hard to reduce our cost in that area to only hit by what we really, really need.

So, I think that's one obvious example. We did quite a bit around travel at different times, and that sort of eased off a bit. Sometimes goes up a bit as well, particularly on the trade related stuff. But on the domestic travel, we've sought to make gains. So I think there are lots of places we seek to make gains, that mean that we don't stop doing all the things that people want us to do, and we don't turn off anything really important, because it is growing, because there are a lot of us, and a budget that's still quite large.

Thank you. Great. Okay, thank you. We have a question from Scott Lewis. Thank you. And just coming back to the Primary Sector Growth Fund, can the minister confirm that projects that are expected to increase greenhouse gas emissions, freshwater pollution, nutrient losses, irrigation demand, or stocking rates are eligible for funding from the Primary Sector Growth Fund?

Sorry, I don't understand your question. Explain that further for me. Sorry. It looks like you're just reading it. Tell me what it means. Yeah, so effectively, from what I've heard earlier, you're confirming that there's not really the environmental concerns for the whole primary sector growth. But we're open to everything. So what I'm asking you to confirm is that those things that could be funded may well include freshwater issues, may well include- Greater climate pollution. I'm just seeking some clarification.

Well, I think when we finish here this week and we all get in our cars and go to the airport, we may well contribute to climate change. So we can't divorce our- So it is climate pollution... it's not a yes, we can't divorce ourselves from all these things completely. But the land use flexibility project we've announced i- is very clearly around ways to increase production whilst meeting and improving our environment and climate obligations. And so, no, I'm not going to answer the question the way that you've put it, but what I would say is actually the Primary

Sector Growth Fund is very, very clear, and it is helping us to improve agricultural production in New Zealand, whilst at the same time within that and across government, we look to reduce, to meet our obligations. So we can't compartmentalize one part of our activity, when from Ag Zero to many other areas of government we have funding that achieves many of the things that you are asking about.

Christine. I think we've got a sup from Jenna. Can I have a sup on that? Because, Minister, because there's... How will the work that's being done then in terms of Hill Country erosion projects, the catchment committees, the QEII investment, help with the future climate adaptation work that New Zealand- Yeah. Well, look, I think that's a very good point.

And so there are a number of areas where we have a response. Equally, in doing that, some people would argue you've doubled the amount of QE funding, that's not helping to grow productivity. The point that I'm making is we're able to do many things at the same time, with different amounts of funding. Ray, somebody will help me with the amount we put into hill erosion and so on, because it is a very, very big challenge in parts of New Zealand.

The government has a role to play for that. If you think about Tairawhiti all the way down through- Yeah... all the way down through, um- 27.8 Yeah, 27.8 million. 6.3 a year. Yeah. 6.3. 6.3 a year, so over that period of time- Yep... over the four years. And we see that we have a role to play in that because you get better environmental outcomes. And so, you'll always be able to find one budget line that doesn't achieve all the things that everyone in Parliament would want. But across the $1.33 billion, including the significant amount of resource that

the taxpayer puts into Ag Zero around climate change, we are partnering with the sector and others to come up with the solutions they need as we would like them to, in an environmental way, produce more so we can sell more overseas. Bit of sup. Sup. Yes. So how can we be sure, how can the ministry assure us that the Primary Sector Growth Fund is going to be consistent with New Zealand's emissions reduction budgets?

Very hefty. I think we'll start with the minister wise. Right. The Primary Sector Growth Fund's not trying to solve all the problems. So Ag Zero or the investments we've got on climate change mitigation, which are sort of close to about $100 million a year in the budget, whether it's our investments with the Ag Emissions Center or with Ag Zero or others that we're undertaking, is a vast investment combined with industry that is going after methane mitigations. So there are currently an investment in around 13 mitigations that will run to 17. Some of those will come on stream this year in New

Zealand, and will have significant knockdown of methane effects. So if the bolus for one-year-old animals, comes to market in the latter part of this year, which is what we're expecting, that is a potential 70% knockdown in methane. So, if you take then there are two probiotics, I visited the companies that are producing these in the United States.

Yes. They look to have a very positive effect on methane mitigation, maybe in the 40% mark. There are two vaccines that have been developed, one in New Zealand, one in the United States, that will knock down methane. So we've got significant investments in those. There's also investments we've got in genetics. So, sheep genetics are already available, but there are a range of genetics in the dairy herd and beef herd as well that are under development.

And then there is pastures, and what you heard, pastures. So on the issue of solving methane, we will more than solve the current problem on methane. I'm very confident about that. We're the only country in the world that has invested, collectively in the way that we have, the envy of most that I visit, for the fact we've actually managed to get private and public funds together to go after a solution for this. Because that is protecting the thing that we are best in the world at, which is primary sector production, and it's

where we trade, and it's where we make our money as a country, so we need to look after that. On the Primary Sector Growth Fund, the initiatives that we announced last week, were particularly important because, the investment, while we're trying to see growth in production, those investments are very much about finding the markers of environmental quality in that investment. So the companies, just as we are, want to make sure that, as we look to grow, that we can actually test out in commercial farming systems all of the available

mitigations. One of the things you see in farming systems is you may have on your farm some mitigation, and I might have some, but neither of us have stacked it all together or even tried for more. And so what we're not seeing in all of the markets is kind of what is truly possible if you get all of the mitigations lined up. And so it's really an attempt to speed up the application of the available science, while we look for new science about reducing nutrient loss, as well as kind of improving on the methane side.

So I'm confident that both those programs are sitting pretty well together. And on the methane side, I'm very confident that we'll have a solution not just in New Zealand- But, but I reckon we'll have a solution for the rest of the world that's got extensive farming systems. Okay. That's it. Thank you. Can I ask a supplement? Yes, Maureen, go. Just a quick one on that- It was a slightly different- ... just to follow up from what you've said. Yeah, sure.

So in terms of the R&D that's going on into mitigation, how will you assess then, once it's rolled out, what the uptake is and what the impact is? Oh, totally. So, there are two things. In terms of the impact, you have to get into the national inventory.

So, if you develop a product and we license it, and we say, "Yes, it's safe to use, it's safe for animals, safe for any human consumption. There's no residues of the product," and so on. Mm. That's the first test. Then when the farmers use it, the second test that we have is it gets into our national inventory. So EcoPond is an example of a New Zealand-developed product which knocks down methane in settling ponds by around 97%. That takes out about 6% of dairy farm emissions of methane.

That's fully tested, the science is proven, and it's in our national inventory, which means it's recognized worldwide. So any dairy farmer that puts an EcoPond in it gets a 6% gain on their emissions profile, and New Zealand gets that gain. So, that's how we measure it. And of course, the minister and prime minister announced the early adoption fund last week at Fieldays, because we recognize that farmers need to get comfortable with using new technologies.

And we want to speed up the rate of application of those technologies, so we'll be following that closely. That fund matches private... So if you come forward as a private sector business company or large farming producer, with funding, then the government can match it up to $17 million per year for the next three years. So that's just to speed up the application rate.

Thank you. Sorry, just a bit of clarification. Yes. So, in terms of the uptake, so you're relying on farmers- Well, that's what they're-... enrolling- Yeah... in, into the database then? Well, ultimately these things will be able to be measured through a number of places where they're captured, particularly from processing companies, and in the returns that they have to make as well about emissions and their emissions profile. But I think the big challenge we have going forward is not will we have things that can mitigate methane, it's actually getting these things at a price

that meet the farmers' needs, making sure the processing companies are supporting those in a way that allows for them to be pulled through on-farm. Do you want to add something, Julian? And the idea is that you have a range of tools. The government is investing to make sure when they're available to New Zealand, because our production system is fairly unique. There are other countries of the world where the animals are inside or in barns all the time.

Yes. So it's easier for the solutions to be delivered. But equally, we want them, when they're developed, to be available to New Zealand. Whilst sitting in New Zealand, we're very large compared to the rest of the world, and we shouldn't take for granted that the innovation to reduce methane will easily be available in New Zealand.

And secondly, that it will be at a cost that farmers could have access to it without it being prohibitive. And so the reason for investments across a couple of government's ag zero is to achieve that. It's our way of making sure those solutions are there, rather than sitting back and saying, "Well, we have to be net zero by 2050," and ensure the solutions are there actively with the sector investing because, of course, half of the funding is matched by the private sector as well, from the dairy companies to the meat companies, to the banks, to

the fertilizer companies. They are spending their own money, as we are, to make sure those solutions are here. Julie. Just to practically explain it, so we work quite closely with the companies that are developing the technologies. So for example, there's a couple of coated urea projects. The fertilizer companies give us the information on terms of farmer uptake of those products, so we don't need the information from pharma. EcoPond is a similar sort of thing. We work for the company, so we'll get the raw data from the companies, and it'll depend on the technology.

We've got a project with Beef and Lamb New Zealand on a platform called Improve, which tracks sheep genetics. And as those sheep genetics, we see those low methane sheep being sold, we'll be able to track the rollout of those low methane genetics through the herd, and then pick that up in our national inventory. So we'll look, depending on the technology, we'll look for efficient ways of collecting the information around how widely it's being used in our farming systems.

Because we don't want to miss anyone. No, absolutely. We definitely don't want to miss it. So in many ways, it's a proof of concept. Those were the six projects that were announced last week in partnership with the prime sector. Proofs of concept and then that gets rolled out. Yeah. But ultimately, when it comes to emissions reduction, farmers will have the tools and be able to make choices for themselves with their processor as to what best suits their situation and their condition to achieve what they need to.

And the reduction is set in law, but the processors are already doing this in many cases with farmers because they say they need them for their customers overseas. And so rather than a tax, it actually comes by way of incentive that the processors themselves have started to incentivize because they want the change on farm that they then need when they export.

Thank you, minister. We've had another question from Jo Lux. Yeah, thank you. You know how there used to be biscuits? So it'll, it'll, it'll be just a bit-- But, you know, perhaps a good example of a reprioritization of funding was the announcement, uh, last week of the, the $51 million, uh, supporting adoption on farm. So that's a good example where money carry forward over a period of time. Main, the important thing from our perspective is we didn't lose the money and we got a chance to do something with it. So, so fi- finally on that... Go ahead. Finally on that, if you think about that, that adoption part, so some years ago, we could have said, "Well, you know, we should put some money aside to help with adoption because it's in everybody's interest to find ways for farmers to start using the new technologies."

By the way, uh, their use of the technology is commercial. They've paid to get it. Uh, um, companies, uh, themselves promote them. They spend budget to do that. So Ag Zero, uh, is a business that we are 50% owner in, and therefore there will be a return to the taxpayer over time.

So it's, you know, rather than a government money, you should view it just as sort of, uh, I suppose, marketing and promotion for uptake.
