To, what's the term for forests? Silviculture. Silviculture. I'm wine minister also. From silviculture and so on, actually, we need good hands-on practices. But I've got a very, very clear message to anybody who's sitting at home wondering what they should do career-wise.

Agriculture and the primary sector's fantastic, and forestry has great opportunities for the future. Thank you, Minister. Now we've now got Paulo. Minister, we sit in this select committee room with the most wood, and very skillfully processed. My question is, could you expound on the government's initiatives to support the forest industry?

Yeah. And as a sub to that, the higher value wood processing. Yeah. Well, look, that's a very good question. Ultimately, the government doesn't own the trees. We have some of our own, and so I've got to be very careful. I don't want to be in a position where I tell people to do with what they, to tell them what to do with the wood that they produce. We need to support them, but we need to level the playing field.

I am of a view that there is a reason that logs go overseas and not enough value added in New Zealand, and we've been working consistently to try and address that, including changes that would say you get a consent for a wood processing plant more easily, with more certainty, which brings the cost down. We have funds available that help us in some of these areas, which I'm just going to find here so I get it exactly right.

Wood processing teams. Wood processing funds. Wood processing growth fund. Tell us, Sam. So the Wood Processing Growth Fund, Paulo, is an opportunity to reduce the cost and risk of investment and feasibility activity. Good. And equally, improves access to capital for those looking to do large scale. So ultimately, what it's trying to do is to support the opportunity to produce more valued good, value-add products here onshore.

So we are a fiber-rich nation. The opportunity for growth within the forestry and wood product sector is less about growing more. It's about doing more and supporting more markets with value-add product. Mm. Yes. And then at the same time, better market access around the world too. You have this problem when it comes to trade that often your log can go at a very low tariff rate, and as you add value to the wood itself, tariff escalation, tariff rate goes up.

It's prohibitive. So we always look to sort of level the playing field so that when value is added here in New Zealand, it can get access to markets overseas. We've had a body of work underway with Australia around structural timber. I might have spoken about that in the committee before. It's very hard for New Zealand structural timber to be sold into Australia, even though they have a wood deficit they must import from the world, and it's because their standards are different than ours.

And their view is our wood is not strong enough to be structural. Either they're right or we're right. We can't both be right, and our houses are not falling down, so I suspect it's us. But their government and agriculture minister, there's a joint study undertaken by a university to start looking at those. And if we could get that certification or recognition on the strength of New Zealand wood, that would be a significant opportunity for New Zealand.

And the final bit here is part of that land use flexibility project or concept that we were talking about earlier. In forestry, we're putting joint funding into a feasibility on modular building construction, which in itself multiplies the value of the log by almost seven times.

And so- Wow... there will be many things we do around technology and so on to help with this. But adding more value in New Zealand is something that's important. There's another bit though that is less traditional, where I think you will see greater value, and that is the use of the fiber in our forests for energy. Yeah. And there are many, many ideas that are very, very good ideas.

There's talk in parts of New Zealand where people may decide to plant, to have trees promulgated that grow faster, perhaps could sequest more carbon more quickly, and they might cut them down after six or seven years and then use only for energy, as opposed to waiting for 20 years and putting them through a sawmill and the residual is for energy.

These are all good ideas. We've got funding available through our government to support and help with this, although again, I would caution us, there are many, many people out there with great ideas-... and solutions, that they can't attract any private sector capital to. It's actually a partnership we want to do it rather than government doing it alone. But I think you'll see over the next sort of four or five years, for a range of reasons, the fiber that's in the tree valued more greatly than it is at the moment just as a log,

and there will be a significant number of additional uses for it, including I think energy could be a very important part of how we meet our climate obligations and grow greater energy for sufficiency. That was the question. From- Thanks, sir... Rosha and then Dana. Minister, you've talked about adding more value and referred to asset value creation. Can you tell us what that means, what it looks like specifically?

Asset value creation? Well, it'd be a number of things, including more forests that are more mature and the value goes up. But I come back to Maori land as an example. There are parts of the country where trees could well be grown and would add value to the asset of that land straight away. But it's just about enabling some of those choices. With a lot of Maori land, there are obstacles that we need to work through that in other parts of the economy, a bit to land there aren't.

But that would be a very important part of it, I think. Dana. But he didn't get to that. Oh, sorry. Sort of a- There was a reason why I spoke to this... serendipitous something. Okay. Yeah. Well, okay, and then- Does Sap want anything to add to that? No, I think we're good. Well, has MPI got anything to- I'm not rich.

So on- What's your actual question? What do you mean by asset value creation specifically? Where have we said it? Under the Crown Revenues, stuff shifting from active harvesting to asset value creation. Oh, okay. Maturing forests. Yeah. Sorry, forests. The Crown still owns about 30,000 hectares of commercial forestry. So Crown Forestry is a business unit which sits under Te Uru Rākau. We're moving from a phase, commissioner.

Historically, it's been a harvest-focused work program. We're now going back to a growing part of the life cycle. So it's all about value development through subculture. So it's protecting and tending the forest to improve the value of the asset. So it's more about spending money than selling logs right now.

Yeah. Okay. So in some cases, newer forests, you may prune more regularly, so you have stronger, straighter wood that can be used differently. But it's the promotion or attendance to the forest and the age of the forest as well, which would add additional value for the Crown. Okay.

Fancy way of saying growing new trees. Yeah. Well, you grow them better though, too. And supporting the financial statements, which there's less revenue and more expenditure at the moment. My next one when you say- If you just think on that, if you harvest your forest at 15 or 20 years, it has a value. If you leave it for another 10, the value of the forest increases.

Yeah. And those are decisions that we make from a forestry point of view. Yeah. My sub was about wood processing and about the opportunity in India for processed products, i.e., pulp or- Yeah... construction. And I know- Huge... that they were interested, but I wonder if you have any insight into that, because it's a massive building site at the moment in India.

Yeah, look, a huge, huge opportunity into India. We've got 95% of all wood products will go into India, will be at a zero tariff rate from day one, and that's a significant opportunity. In fact, this week, I think, the very first shipment of wood and logs from the South Island is in a ship and on its way to India.

Yeah, cool. And I think it was a deal when I took that forestry mission up there last year. I think it was a deal that was done when they were there, and it just demonstrates there's a lot of interest in what we produce. But from a value-added point of view as well, it's not just logs, although logs will be part of that. I don't have all of the figures in front of me, but we used to be a very important part of wood sales into India. There are a number of reasons why that changed. Then Australia got a free trade agreement, and their

increase in exports of wood products went up hundreds of percent from memory. I'll get the exact figures and give them to you because there's lots of different figures bounced around and ours shrank during that period of time. We've got an opportunity to build that back, but I think there will be chances around value-added rather than only logs going up there, although, again, logs will be an important part of that trade. India's growing very, very quickly.

Construction to manufacturing. They're opening more universities every year than anyone else in the world, more airports every year than anyone else in the world, building more roads and everything else. And so for New Zealand businesses and companies that now have a right to be there and be part of that under the FTA and with a zero tariff rate for almost all of the wood products we produce, then there will be significant opportunity for the New Zealand forestry sector. And this just gives them more options.

As markets around the world diminish or tariffs come up or economies slow, they have options as to where they want to put their product. And I think, in essence, India will become a very important part of the forestry sector. Right. Do you have a sub? Yes. Yes, I do.

On that note, minister, talking about wood processing, I think everyone agrees that we want to extract more value from the forest that we're growing. You've talked about structural, you've talked about energy. Can you give examples of other value options you're looking into, if at all, like perhaps the extraction of chemicals? I am.

What else is being- Yeah... pursued? So some time ago, Scion did some work around that of what else you could take from a New Zealand pine tree. Not a radiata pine tree, but a New Zealand pine tree, because that's what we're calling it from now on. Ultimately, there are many things there.

There is talk around doing much more innovative science in some of these areas. So if you talk to the average forester or others, then generally the view is that you grow a tree, you cut it down, it's turned into a table, a house, or the log is sent overseas or something.

But we know that there are many parts of that tree, in addition to the fiber, will have value. And so, there are many projects of the way. By the way, earlier in the last hearing, we were talking about the Primary Sector Growth Fund. We've ensured that the forestry sector, including processors and others, have the same access to that fund as anybody else does across the primary sector.

It's not just agricultural or horticultural, and good projects will be assessed and funding would be available. But I think the answer is there's a very wide range of things that it can be used for, from the equivalent of turpentine, where there's a company in New Zealand that's looking to develop that in and around Kawerau, to all sorts of other, many other things.

Absolutely. Yes, sure. Minister, you've rightfully acknowledged that there are many people out there with good ideas. Have you considered subsidizing these ideas rather than pursuing partnerships at the risk of projects not being realized because people can't raise the funding needed?

Well, so you've got to be very, very careful because the government only has so much resource, and it's not ours. We take it from the taxpayer, and so largely, whenever we put money into something, generally the government has to be there to socialize risk And so ultimately, if you are socialising risk, you have to be very, very careful on behalf of the taxpayer, because actually that means the greater the risk offered, the greater the subsidy. The less the private sector wants to invest, the greater the likelihood that the taxpayer doesn't get a return or loses their money.

So largely, it is in cooperation, but there are examples where there is... I don't think subsidy is the right word, because subsidy suggests you'd have to do it forever. And in most of our trade agreements, WHO, we don't subsidize things. Others, like the European Union do, and we fight against them all the time. It's like grants. Support. That's where I was heading. Grant. Exactly right, yeah, grants and so on. So, there are support available.

As an example, through the Kanoa, there are grants available that have gone into projects in the forestry industry sector or other types of projects and so on. So it's not just within the budget that we have, there are other parts of government to support that. Are you aware of any grants that are going to pursue some of the new technologies and new opportunities we're talking about?

Yeah. We'll give you example of one, but why don't we come up with a list and send it back in rather than going through. Oh, sorry. I was just about to say, the minister referred to the package of land use flexibility projects. Within that- Yeah... there's a project called VOMO. So that's an $8 million project where the government's putting in 3.8 million. So you highlighted two, what are the other examples?

One was engineered wood products and the other is, as you highlighted, the bioproducts industry. So the VOMO project is an approach which looks at prefabrication of modular mass timber units. So that's manufacturing offsite, using mass timber in a standardized module, which can be rolled out across a number of verticals like schools, student accommodation, hospitals, hotels, anything where there's a repetitive modular kind of thing.

Mm-hmm. And so this is a pretty exciting project which is currently applied in Europe. And the provider believes there's opportunity to roll that out in a New Zealand context. So it's a very innovative approach to construction and a low carbon method of construction.

And around bioenergy, there's a number of projects that are, I mean, at various stages, have been considered, I understand. But again, that's with the Crown. Thank you. Jo, you have a question? Yes, I do. Thank you. I know that there's an over 57 million increase in the capital investment in the Crown's forestry assets appropriation.

Is that for the purchase of new forests and/or planting of new forests? It's MYA. It's a little bit MYA. Yeah. It's just- It needs to be rolled over. Or is it for something totally- It can be rolled over. Yeah. It's- So it's- You go ahead. So that reflects the capital investment in the Crown's forest, the joint venture forest. It's the end of a five-year MYA, so that money is not planned to be spent this year.

The MYA will expire at the end of 2027, and that'll be rolled over into another five-year MYA. So, it's just the timing. It's a technical accounting. Another one of those. Yeah, it's quite. Okay. Thank you. That is all. Thank you.

Good. Madam Chair. Oh, just in the nick of time. I've had my hand up for a little while. Sitting here. I only just saw the wood for the trees. I know. I wanted to stop quickly on what Kushla was raising around investment and research and development, knowing that there's, what, some $400 million being cut across research, across government. Can the officials tell us how much those research dollar cuts will impact on forestry specifically?

Well, look, for the funding that we have in our budget, it still remains there. We move it around from time to time, but I pay particular attention to forestry, and I often argue with the Agriculture Minister to make sure that forestry has its fair share. If you think about Scion as an example, which has dedicated most of the research are all for forestry, it remains there on the campus.

It's come together sort of as part of the primary sector research, but we've sought and received assurances that forestry will continue to be a performer and as far as scientific research is concerned. The site of Scion continues. They have some ideas about what more they'd like to do with it. They're working through that sort of thing, by way of business case at the moment, and some of them are quite, quite exciting, although they're early days.

We will always grow very straight trees in New Zealand. New Zealand pine grows faster here than anywhere else in the world. But as with other parts of the primary sector, we need to find other uses and greater value from what's inside the tree than just the log itself. And the only way to do that is through science.

And so, we'll keep ensuring that the funding is available. In many cases where things come to us that are outside of, I'm not responsible for the part that you're talking about, but where we have funding available and projects through the Primary Sector Growth Fund, forestry gets its fair share as well.

I think in the next few years, you'll hear much more from the private sector in cooperation with the government about new uses, not of wood, but of the tree itself. And some of those have the ability to create significant value, not only for foresters and landowners, but more broadly for the New Zealand economy.

Kushla. Thank you. Minister, you have announced a $27.8 million fund for erosion control between July 27 and June 31. When can we hear what projects will be funded? Yeah. Well, so firstly, it's very important because there are parts of the country where we have a lot of erosion. Some of this is forestry, but other parts, of course, is around farms. We sort of take a similar approach to all of that. And the work and detail that's going on as to where it might best be spent is probably less a forestry one, but I don't know if there's someone else

here who can speak to it. Road control, yeah. The evaluation of that is actually starting today. So, it's the second four-year tranche of funding. We recognize that council's landowners need certainty. And so whilst we've still got a year of the first four-year tranche, we're trying to start early on that second four-year tranche to ensure there's continuity. So, decisions will be presented for the minister towards the end, in the next three to six months.

Can you give an update on any current active erosion projects? Can I give an update on any active current erosion projects? Maybe a good one would be right now, GDC, $800,000 under the first four-year program. And so that $800,000 goes towards capacity within council for land management, capability and capacity.

It goes towards supporting erosion plans on farm, and it also goes towards supporting poplar pole planting. So, as you know, it's oversubscribed. There's going to be- Mm... more ask than what's available- Mm... but it's a very meaningful fund. Yeah, because once that money's spent on the capacity and the plan, is there going to be money left for the execution?

Well, every council comes with a different set of proposals around what they need in their region. So we don't prescribe that. Horizons have a different approach where they have their own poplar nurseries, and the way that they do it is they use a third of the funding from the Crown, a third of their own funding, and the landowner pays a third to actually buy the poplar poles.

So there's different approaches. It's not a standardized approach. Is there a commitment to- Through you, Chair. Question. Yeah Sorry. Sorry, Madam Chair. That's okay. Is there a commitment to continue or prioritize funding for people who have already received funding to develop capacity and plans? Well, we'll look on that. It's on a case-by-case basis.

There's no decision not to, but we also want to broaden out those that may need access to funding and so on. So those are things that'll be brought to me to consider. In some cases, you may well find those who've got funding need less of it or want more of it or want to change what they're doing. And so it's four years it rolls over, but on a case-by-case basis, it's made. There's no decision to say it could be taken away from anybody. Evaluations and schemes need to be able to take place and then prioritization about what more they mainly may do around erosion.

Thank you. Okay. Okay. Thanks. Thanks, Madam Chair. It would be remiss of me not to ask a question about the East Coast. Minister, how do you intend to reduce risks from forests planted throughout the East Coast for erosion control, that are no longer viable for harvesting?

Well, you will have seen that the council has done a lot of work in that area. There was a report done under the previous government, which we inherited, that needs to be worked through, firstly. Secondly, the changes to the NES for commercial forestry move very much to a risk-based approach. There'll be areas where there are greater and more stringent rules required than some other parts where actually there is less required based upon risk. So that's to set up the framework for country.

In the Tairāwhiti region, there continue to be significant challenges. And so the way to do this is for the council, with the Crown, to work with landowners and foresters to find the very best way to achieve the outcomes that we need. I've got to say that, again, as I said earlier, I think the council has a very challenging job because it was a significant weather event.

And there have been reports that have been done previously, previous government that I've inherited, that draw attention to and ask questions about the capacity and capability. This is not a reflection necessarily on individuals in the council, it is more to do with the significance of the scale of the event and the work that needs to be done to rectify the land use change is a very important part of that.

I have to say that, that I am disappointed that the Gisborne Council seem to have decided that the only real tool that they have or want to use is that of enforcement, because there are examples of people who are exemplary in their approach to forestry. They are doing things they don't need to outside of their consent. They are spending their own money and, or inviting council out to come and talk to them about what needs to be done, and there seems to be a fairly heavy-handed approach to enforcement.

Although, albeit, I accept enforcement is an important tool. I think the best way to get the best outcome for Tairāwhiti is for the council to work closely with all landowners and interested parties on an almost a case-by-case basis on what the solutions will be there. There are examples where people have another 10 years before they have to log, and they've been told that actually they need to get rid of their forest today. And ultimately, the way they've been asked to get rid of their forest, they would view as challenging for the environment.

So I've asked officials to engage deeply with the council, provide them the support they need. Coming back to the point of the report that was generated for the previous government, drawing attention to the challenges around capacity and capability of the council. And I think it's in everybody's interest to find ways forward on that. Mm. Speaking of the official support, if people think, for example, the only way out for them to survive is carbon, is there any support Being provided from government to ensure we don't create future

hazards for when trees come to the end of lifecycle. How do you mean by the only way out is carbon? Well, if people are grappling up the coast at the moment and trying to think what they can do, like you've said, some of them- I know... feel like they're going to have to remove their forests. For some to stay viable, they're looking at all the options. I see. If they decide their only option is carbon-... what support can they expect-... in the future?

So if you step back from support, the only reason in the future they wouldn't have the ability to keep forest or to have challenges on their land is if the rules changed. So if you think about much of the wood that's come down to weather events, some of that is legacy wood. It is from logging many years ago based on the rules of the time.

So we have to get the rules right. That's the reason, both with the RMA replacement, but the changes to the National Environment Standard for commercial forestry, we are looking at a rules-based approach. One of the things we're saying is this is the standard for the following reasons. Councils are able to go further than that standard should they want, but it has to be based upon evidence.

So I think what you've seen in the past is decisions are made for a range of reasons that probably aren't based on evidence always and, as a result, we end up, in the future, dealing with the consequences. So what we're trying to do across forestry, as well as agriculture and horticulture, everything in the private sector, is to create certainty and try and get a regulatory framework in place that is easily understood, it's not heavy and bureaucratic, based upon risk, and achieves what we need to. What we need in Tairāwhiti, every part of New

Zealand, is a forestry industry that is responsible, and they meet their obligations, and that actually the rules support them. But what we're seeing far too often, as a result of weather events, and I can understand the reason why, is a move towards rules that actually are prohibitive and restrictive, but in itself may not achieve what a council would want. So the only thing I can say what support would be available is we're going to keep working very hard with the councils, the experts, and the forestry sector themselves, because they know as much about

growing trees as what happens on their land as anybody else does, how we come up with the rules that are fit for purpose, and in the case of your question, can stand the test of time. That won't be done in one day or one week, and it'll continue to take a lot of effort. But we actually, for Tairāwhiti and many other parts of New Zealand, need to get the rules right, because without forestry on land that can be forested, then there would be a very big hole in the local economy.

And I have heard the mayor say that she wants investment in wood processing in the area, which I agree with. But some of the things the council at the moment is driving people away from Tairāwhiti, not towards it. So I want to work with them to get the balance right. Thank you. Thank you very much, Minister. You're welcome. Thank you.
Thank you.
